edges.description: Cytotoxic T-cell adhesion and effector polarization injure microvascular endothelium.
Occlusive microangiopathy affects retinal branch arterioles.
Occlusive microangiopathy produces multifocal brain ischemic lesions.
Endothelial injury and microhemorrhages contribute to brain tissue damage.
Humoral autoimmunity may contribute to endothelial injury in a subset of patients.
Cerebral lesions produce encephalopathy and neurocognitive symptoms.
Cerebral involvement commonly affects executive function and recall.
Cerebral involvement can include psychiatric or behavioral manifestations.
Branch retinal artery occlusions are the defining retinal manifestation.
Retinal ischemia can produce localized visual field loss.
Cochlear ischemic injury leads to sensorineural hearing impairment.
Vestibular involvement can produce vertigo or imbalance.

nodes.description: Aspirin is described as an adjunctive antiplatelet agent in Susac syndrome, but published treatment-review evidence explicitly notes that efficacy data are scant.

evidence.reference_title: Treatment of Susac Syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Aspirin may be used as an adjunctive agent, although evidence showing efficacy is scant.

nodes.description: Branch retinal artery occlusion is the defining ocular manifestation and may be symptomatic or detected only by ophthalmic testing.

evidence.reference_title: Clinical Characterization and Prognostic Risk Factors of Susac Syndrome: A Retrospective Multicenter Study.
evidence.snippet: Susac syndrome (SuS) is a rare disorder characterized by encephalopathy, branch retinal artery occlusion, and sensorineural hearing loss, often accompanied by vertigo.

nodes.description: Activated cytotoxic CD8-positive T cells adhere to CNS microvessels and polarize cytotoxic granules toward endothelial targets, supporting a proximal T-cell-mediated injury mechanism.

evidence.reference_title: CD8(+) T cell-mediated endotheliopathy is a targetable mechanism of neuro-inflammation in Susac syndrome.
evidence.snippet: By investigating immune cells from the blood, cerebrospinal fluid, and CNS of SuS patients, we demonstrate oligoclonal expansion of terminally differentiated activated cytotoxic CD8+ T cells (CTLs).

evidence.reference_title: CD8(+) T cell-mediated endotheliopathy is a targetable mechanism of neuro-inflammation in Susac syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Our study identifies CD8+ T-cell-mediated endotheliopathy as a key disease mechanism in SuS and highlights therapeutic opportunities.

nodes.description: Microangiopathic vascular injury produces multifocal brain lesions, especially corpus callosum and peri-callosal lesions, leading to encephalopathy, headache, cognitive dysfunction, and focal deficits.

evidence.reference_title: Susac syndrome: A scoping review.
evidence.snippet: subacute encephalopathy with unusual headache and pseudo-psychiatric features associated with multifocal ischemic white matter, grey matter nuclei and specifically corpus callosum lesions along with leptomeningeal enhancement on brain MRI

nodes.description: Microvascular injury of the cochlea and vestibular apparatus produces sensorineural hearing impairment, tinnitus, vertigo, and imbalance.

evidence.reference_title: Susac syndrome: A scoping review.
evidence.snippet: cochleo-vestibular damage with neurosensorial hearing loss predominating on low frequencies.

nodes.description: Cognitive deficits can range from subtle to profound, with frequent involvement of executive function and short-term recall.

evidence.reference_title: The neurocognitive and neuropsychiatric manifestations of Susac syndrome: a brief review of the literature and future directions.
evidence.snippet: Executive function and short-term recall are affected frequently.

nodes.description: Encephalopathy is one arm of the classic clinical triad and may present with confusion, cognitive dysfunction, headache, behavioral change, or focal neurologic symptoms.

evidence.reference_title: Clinical Characterization and Prognostic Risk Factors of Susac Syndrome: A Retrospective Multicenter Study.
evidence.snippet: Susac syndrome (SuS) is a rare disorder characterized by encephalopathy, branch retinal artery occlusion, and sensorineural hearing loss, often accompanied by vertigo.

nodes.description: High-dose corticosteroids are commonly used as first-line induction therapy, especially for active CNS disease, but monotherapy may be insufficient in severe encephalopathic disease.

evidence.reference_title: Susac syndrome: A scoping review.
evidence.snippet: First-line treatment mostly consists of high dose corticosteroids.

nodes.description: A subset of patients has anti-endothelial cell antibodies, including complement-activating IgG1, suggesting parallel or amplifying humoral autoimmunity against the microvasculature.

evidence.reference_title: Clinical, paraclinical and serological findings in Susac syndrome: an international multicenter study.
evidence.snippet: Recently, a role for anti-endothelial cell antibodies (AECA) in SuS has been proposed.

evidence.reference_title: Clinical, paraclinical and serological findings in Susac syndrome: an international multicenter study.
evidence.snippet: In all seropositive cases, AECA belonged to the complement-activating IgG1 subclass.

nodes.description: Susac syndrome is centered on immune-mediated injury of small-vessel endothelium in the brain, retina, and inner ear, producing occlusive microangiopathy rather than primary demyelination.

evidence.reference_title: Susac syndrome: neurological update (clinical features, long-term observational follow-up and management of sixteen patients).
evidence.snippet: Susac syndrome is a likely autoimmune microangiopathy affecting the brain, retina and inner ear.

evidence.reference_title: Susac syndrome: A scoping review.
evidence.snippet: Susac syndrome is a rare disease characterized by an inflammatory microangiopathy limited to the brain, eye, and ear vessels.

nodes.description: IVIG is used as adjunctive induction, relapse treatment, or maintenance in refractory or relapsing Susac syndrome.

evidence.reference_title: Treatment of Susac Syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Additional agents, including intravenous immunoglobulins, intravenous cyclophosphamide, or rituximab are often necessary to induce a sustained remission.

nodes.description: Natalizumab has been reported as an adjunctive therapy targeting alpha-4-integrin-mediated T-cell adhesion in severe Susac syndrome, but the human evidence is limited to a small treated case subset alongside other therapy.

evidence.reference_title: CD8+ T cell-mediated endotheliopathy is a targetable mechanism of neuro-inflammation in Susac syndrome.
evidence.snippet: disease severity decreases in four SuS patients treated with natalizumab along with other therapy.

nodes.description: Psychiatric symptoms may occur during Susac syndrome, including anxiety, mood disorder symptoms, or psychosis, although attribution can be complicated by treatment effects.

evidence.reference_title: The neurocognitive and neuropsychiatric manifestations of Susac syndrome: a brief review of the literature and future directions.
evidence.snippet: Psychiatric manifestations may be absent or may include anxiety, mood disorders or psychosis.

nodes.description: Occlusion of retinal branch arterioles causes retinal ischemia, scotomas, and visual field defects; fluorescein angiography may show arterial wall hyperfluorescence even when symptoms are subtle.

evidence.reference_title: Susac syndrome: A scoping review.
evidence.snippet: ophthalmological involvement that may be pauci-symptomatic, with bilateral occlusions of the branches of the central artery of the retina at fundoscopy and arterial wall hyperfluorescence on fluorescein angiography

nodes.description: Retinal branch ischemia can cause focal visual field loss perceived as a scotoma.

evidence.reference_title: Clinical, paraclinical and serological findings in Susac syndrome: an international multicenter study.
evidence.snippet: Susac syndrome (SuS) is a rare disorder thought to be caused by autoimmune-mediated occlusions of microvessels in the brain, retina and inner ear leading to central nervous system (CNS) dysfunction, visual disturbances due to branch retinal artery occlusions (BRAO), and hearing deficits.

nodes.description: Sensorineural hearing impairment is a defining inner-ear manifestation and may involve low frequencies, although high-frequency loss does not exclude the diagnosis.

evidence.reference_title: Clinical Characterization and Prognostic Risk Factors of Susac Syndrome: A Retrospective Multicenter Study.
evidence.snippet: At disease onset, hearing loss excluding low frequencies occurred in 46.7%.

nodes.description: Steroid-sparing immunosuppressive therapies, including mycophenolate, cyclophosphamide, azathioprine, and rituximab, are used for relapsing, refractory, or severe disease.

evidence.reference_title: Susac syndrome: A scoping review.
evidence.snippet: In refractory patients or in case of relapse, immunomodulatory molecules such as intravenous immunoglobulins or immunosuppressive drugs such as mycophenolate mofetil, cyclophosphamide or rituximab should be started.

nodes.description: Vertigo can occur with vestibulocochlear involvement.

evidence.reference_title: Clinical Characterization and Prognostic Risk Factors of Susac Syndrome: A Retrospective Multicenter Study.
evidence.snippet: Susac syndrome (SuS) is a rare disorder characterized by encephalopathy, branch retinal artery occlusion, and sensorineural hearing loss, often accompanied by vertigo.
